text: This next case is a laparoscopic removal of a left ovary secondary to endometriosis.
In this ultrasound picture you can see a left ovary with endometriomas.
The video shows endometriosis implants visibly located on the surface of the liver and within the pelvic region.
The laparoscopic view shows the left ovary with multiple cysts, consistent with the ultrasound findings. The ovary is prepared for removal due to recurrent endometriosis.
This particular patient she had undergone a hysterectomy in addition to a right ovary removal secondary to endometriosis and pelvic pain.
At the time all the endometriosis that was visible was removed
She had a peritoneal stripping as well as endometriosis excision and as you can see the nature of endometriosis can be chronic and recurrent.
In her case she presented again with endometriosis of her ovary as well as endometriosis in other areas of her pelvis and liver which were not seen before.
The surgeon inspects the ureter, visually tracing its path from the kidney down to the bladder.
The surgeon observes an implant of endometriosis on the right pelvis, in the area where the ovary had been previously removed.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to spread the pelvic tissue, facilitating the visualization and manipulation of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses Maryland bipolar forceps, which allow for both grasping and cauterizing tissue as needed during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon inspects the right ureter, visually tracing its course from the kidney to the bladder, before proceeding with the removal of an endometrial implant located in the pelvis.
The surgeon visually identifies areas of endometriosis located at the posterior aspect of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic hook to ablate an implant of endometriosis located in the right pelvis, where the ovary had been previously removed.
The surgeon identifies additional areas of endometriosis located at the left pelvic brim near the descending colon, visually inspecting these regions for further treatment.

The surgeon navigates near pulsating vascular structures located to the left of the endometrial implants, with the descending colon visible to the right. Careful application of energy is required due to the proximity of these structures.
The surgeon inspects additional areas for endometrial implants and ablates them using surgical instruments, focusing on precise removal of the affected tissue.
The video shows the urocycal ligament suspension of the vagina, with the archway of the ligament visibly supporting the vaginal structure.

The surgeon grasps the left ovary, preparing to drain it to reduce its size for removal through the umbilicus. An endometrioma, known as a chocolate cyst due to its appearance resembling liquid chocolate, is visible.
The surgeon drains the endometrioma from the left ovary, revealing the dark, chocolate-like fluid characteristic of this type of cyst.
The assistant uses a suction irrigator to remove the drained contents of the chocolate cyst from the surgical field, ensuring the area is clear of residual fluid.
The surgeon identifies three to four chocolate cysts, or endometriomas, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon places the decompressed ovary, previously drained of cysts, into an endo pouch. The pouch is then used to facilitate the removal of the ovary through the belly button, avoiding the need for a larger incision.
Following the removal of the ovary, the surgeon inspects the interior of the bladder, revealing an endometriosis implant visible on the bladder wall.

In summary, endometriosis is a chronic and recurrent disease that does require long-term management. As you can see in this case, with recurrent endometriosis despite removal of her uterus, of her right ovary, removal of endometriosis in her previous surgeries, and then you see recurrence again requiring repeated surgeries.

in about ten percent of cases most cases after removal of the uterus in one of the ovaries allows for enough symptom relief that most people don't require another surgery thank you for watching this video and please share with anyone who may have endometriosis or pelvic pain because as you know knowledge is never wasted
